abstract:
This contribution is structured as an academic letter addressed to the late Professor Johan Adam Heyns (1928–1994), focusing specifically on his period as a senior lecturer at Stellenbosch University from 1966 to 1970. Positioned within a broader series of "academic letters" to deceased colleagues, the article utilizes this dialogical genre to facilitate a direct engagement with Heyns’s intellectual legacy during a pivotal decade in South African history. The author re-evaluates Heyns’s early work—primarily centered on apologetics and ethics—against the backdrop of the socio-political implementation of apartheid and the intellectual challenges of secularization.

The study employs a "sixfold mirror" to analyze the developmental stages of Heyns’s career, examining the productive tensions between orthodoxy and modernity, the philosophical influence of Henk Stoker versus the theological tradition of Herman Bavinck, and Heyns’s shifting stance relative to the apartheid struggle. By looking back at the "Stellenbosch Heyns" through the lens of Heyns’s later mature work in Pretoria, the author reflects on the broader trajectory of academic careers. The article contrasts the rigorous, often risk-laden energy of youthful academic production with the wisdom and occasional rigidity that accompanies seniority, ultimately offering a nuanced reappraisal of Heyns’s contribution to Reformed theology in South Africa.
